title: Chapter 9 — Advanced Data Collection Techniques
objectives: Explain when traditional collection pipelines hit scale, speed, and heterogeneity limits
Design crowdsourcing campaigns with clear tasks, quality controls, and ethical labor practices
Compare MTurk and alternative crowd platforms for microtasks and research recruitment
Describe IoT sensing components and edge computing benefits for continuous collection
Anticipate IoT security, privacy, scalability, and data-overload challenges
Characterize big data volume, velocity, variety, and veracity for collection design
Distinguish streaming ingestion, data lakes, and data warehouses for operational storage
Connect advanced collection patterns to real-time sentiment, distributed storage, and AI pipelines

parts.title: From fundamentals to advanced collection
parts.bridge_in: Chapter 2 covered surveys, sampling, and small-scale collection. This chapter asks what changes when evidence must arrive continuously, from many devices, or at volumes no spreadsheet can hold. This opening part motivates advanced techniques—crowdsourcing, IoT sensing, and streaming pipelines—without abandoning decision-focused collection design.

parts.title: Crowdsourcing use cases
parts.bridge_in: When human judgment must scale beyond a small expert team, crowdsourcing distributes microtasks to many workers. This part defines crowdsourcing and shows use cases from fraud review and innovation contests to image tagging on crowd platforms.

parts.title: MTurk and other crowd platforms
parts.bridge_in: Use cases need operational platforms. This part walks the Amazon Mechanical Turk workflow for posting and reviewing HITs, then compares Prolific, CrowdFlower, and Clickworker as alternatives for research recruitment and microtask labor.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:9.2 focusing on platforms.

parts.title: Crowdsourcing quality and ethics
parts.bridge_in: Platforms alone do not guarantee usable labels. This part covers advantages and failure modes of crowdsourcing, best practices for instructions and quality checks, and ethical obligations around fair pay, privacy, and harmful content.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:9.2 covering advantages, challenges, practices, and ethics.

parts.title: IoT components and sensing
parts.bridge_in: Crowdsourcing scales human labeling; IoT scales continuous measurement. This part introduces Internet of Things components—sensors, connectivity, and gateways—with wearables, industrial monitors, and agricultural soil networks as motivating examples.

parts.title: Edge computing and the IoT pipeline
parts.bridge_in: Raw sensor streams overwhelm networks if everything ships to the cloud. This part describes the IoT collection pipeline and the benefits of edge processing for latency, bandwidth, and local decisions such as thermostats, traffic lights, and logistics rerouting.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:9.3 on pipelines and edge benefits.

parts.title: IoT security, privacy, and overload
parts.bridge_in: Always-on sensors create always-on risks. This part covers scalability, security, and privacy challenges in IoT collection, smart-city multi-source designs, and ethical concerns including consent and managing data overload.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:9.3 on challenges, security, and ethics.

parts.title: Big data and streaming
parts.bridge_in: IoT and social platforms produce volume and velocity that traditional databases cannot absorb alone. This part defines big-data characteristics, filtering noisy streams, and distributed systems that support continuous stock, sensor, and social feeds.

parts.title: Big-data tools, lakes, and warehouses
parts.bridge_in: Streaming needs a landing place. This part surveys collection challenges and tooling, then contrasts data lakes for mixed raw sources with data warehouses for structured business intelligence.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:9.4 on tools and storage patterns.

parts.title: Sentiment case study and big-data outlook
parts.bridge_in: The chapter closes by tying advanced collection to an end-to-end application. This final part walks real-time social sentiment analysis, distributed file systems and ingestion modes, then quality, privacy, AI integration, and emerging trends that prepare later storage and modeling chapters.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:9.4 covering the case study, HDFS/ingestion, quality, and outlook.
